D10 Imaging L3 Apply Reference

Aperture & Depth of Field

a wide aperture lets a single distance be sharp and dissolves everything else; a tiny aperture pulls a long range into focus at the cost of light

The aperture is an iris that sets how big the lens opening is. Wide open (a small f-number such as f/1.4), the cone of light that misses the focused plane lands as a big blur disc — only one slim slice of the scene reads sharp. Stopped down (a large f-number such as f/16), the cone shrinks and the depth of field grows; near and far points alike come into focus.

Drag the aperture and watch the background dissolve or sharpen while the focused subject stays put. The grasses and trees behind the flower go from a wash to clear blades as you stop down — the cost is less light reaching the sensor.
